one_line_takeaway: Applying a new 11-principle TLA+ security model (AASM) and two-phase conformance checker (AgentConform) to MCP/A2A/ANP/ACP finds that only transport security is consistently complete across these protocols, with 33 spec-level and 42 implementation-level violations, and that composing protocols through shared bridges breaks 20 of 21 checked composition-safety invariants -- including a demonstrated MCP-prompt-injection-to-A2A-delegation-amplification attack chain invisible to single-protocol analysis.
key_finding: APS scoring (Table 1/Figure 1): across MCP, A2A, ANP, ACP, only L1 Transport Security passes for all four protocols (4/4); L2 Message Transport passes 2/4; L3 Session & Resilience, L4 Identity & Trust, L5 Semantic Operations & Consent, and L6 Audit & Accountability each pass 0/4 -- every protocol has gaps in at least 4 of the 6 layers. Phase 1 (spec-level, via TLC): model-checking 5 protocols against 11 AASM invariants yields a 5x11 matrix with 33 spec-level violations reported. Phase 2 (implementation-level replay): 42 tests executed across reference SDK implementations and official reference servers, confirming violations at the implementation level (the paper states some confirmed findings are under coordinated disclosure and withheld from this preprint). Composition Safety: across 5 composed TLA+ models instantiating 21 CS invariants total, TLC finds violations in 20 of the 21 -- only one invariant (domain isolation in the federated-delegation model) holds by construction; the chained-server composition pattern is reported as violating all five of its invariants (including credential forwarding and transitive trust collapse) and is called the highest-risk pattern because it is the most widely deployed. The paper's headline qualitative finding is a described (not yet fully evaluated in this preprint) cross-protocol attack chain: a prompt-injection-tainted MCP tool output propagates through a shared bridge/conductor to amplify A2A delegation beyond its original scope, with the bridge operation falling outside both protocols' audit domains -- illustrated abstractly in the paper's Figure 3. Per-protocol notes from the text: ACP-Cap is described as failing on 4 of 7 checked AASM principles and as 'closest to completeness' among the studied protocols; ANP is described as the baseline for maximum underspecification and is stated to account for the highest proportion of Ambiguity-Fail findings due to its white-paper status.
core_contribution: The paper (Zheng and Zhang, arXiv:2603.23801) makes three original contributions of its own: (1) the Agent Protocol Stack (APS), a 6-layer architectural model (L1 Transport Security, L2 Message Transport & Wire Format, L3 Session & Resilience, L4 Identity/Capability/Trust, L5 Semantic Operations & Consent, L6 Audit & Accountability) used to score protocol completeness across MCP, A2A, ANP, and ACP; (2) the Agent-Agnostic Security Model (AASM), 11 security principles (P1-P8 core security properties plus WF/SL/CS completeness properties) formalized as TLA+ invariants, each tagged with a 4-way property taxonomy (spec-mandated / spec-recommended / aasm-hardening / aps-completeness) that the authors say distinguishes genuine protocol non-conformance from framework-imposed hardening requirements; and (3) AgentConform, a two-phase conformance-testing pipeline that (i) extracts normative clauses from protocol specs into a typed Protocol IR with explicit Protocol/Environment/Adversary action separation, (ii) compiles the IR into TLA+ models checked against the 11 AASM invariants with the TLC model checker, and (iii) replays TLC counterexample traces as executable tests against live SDK implementations and reference servers. The paper also introduces Composition Safety (CS) as a named, formally modeled principle covering security-property breakage when protocols are bridged (e.g., MCP+A2A via a conductor/proxy).
threat_model: The authors extend the classical Dolev-Yao network adversary (intercept/modify/replay/inject messages) with three LLM/agent-specific adversary capabilities, each modeled as an explicit TLA+ Adversary action separated from Protocol and Environment actions: ADV-1 Prompt Injection (adversary injects control directives into tool outputs that the LLM interprets as instructions -- the paper notes MCP's spec says servers 'must sanitize tool outputs' but the reference SDK performs zero sanitization); ADV-2 Capability Inflation (adversary forges or inflates capability manifests, e.g. MCP tool declarations or A2A agent cards, exploiting the absence of cryptographic attestation on manifests); and ADV-3 Delegation Amplification (adversary exploits transitive delegation in A2A to re-delegate a capability beyond the scope originally granted, since no protocol enforces delegation monotonicity). The paper is explicit that side-channel attacks, timing attacks, and physical access are out of scope.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Section 8.2): (1) Bounded model checking -- TLC explores a finite state space with small bounds (2-3 agents, 2 capabilities), so counterexamples found are valid but absence of a violation does not guarantee correctness at larger scale. (2) Specification fidelity -- the IR extraction pipeline depends on human interpretation of protocol documentation and is not fully automated, which the taxonomy's model-fail category is meant to catch but does not eliminate. (3) Protocol drift -- results are tied to specific specification snapshots (agent protocols evolve rapidly) and should be read as versioned evidence rather than durable claims. (4) Adversary model scope -- the Dolev-Yao + ADV-1/2/3 model targets protocol-level threats only; side-channel, timing, and physical-access attacks are explicitly out of scope. Additionally, the paper is explicit that it is a preprint withholding some material: confirmed implementation-level findings are under coordinated disclosure, and 'full evaluation details will be released in the complete version' -- meaning the 33 spec-level and 42 implementation-level test results, and the composition-safety findings, are reported at a summary/count level in this version without full counterexample traces or a breakdown by individual protocol/principle.

quantitative_results: APS layer pass rates across MCP/A2A/ANP/ACP: L1 Transport Security 4/4, L2 Message Transport 2/4, L3 Session & Resilience 0/4, L4 Identity & Trust 0/4, L5 Semantic Ops & Consent 0/4, L6 Audit & Accountability 0/4
MCP normative-clause extraction: 37 normative clauses extracted from 8 specification documents
Phase 1 (TLC spec-level model checking): 5 protocols x 11 AASM invariants matrix yielding 33 spec-level violations
Phase 2 (implementation replay): 42 tests executed across reference SDK implementations (two protocols with mature Python SDKs) and official reference servers
MCP adapter: 7 protocol actions mapped to JSON-RPC calls (initialize, tools/list, tools/call, shutdown)
A2A adapter: 6 protocol actions mapped to HTTP calls (agent discovery, task send, delegation, consent)
Composition Safety: 5 composed TLA+ models instantiating 21 CS invariants total, with violations found in 20 of 21 (only federated-model domain isolation holds by construction)
Chained-server composition pattern: all 5 of its CS invariants violated (including credential forwarding and transitive trust collapse)
ACP-Cap reported to fail on 4 of 7 checked AASM principles
